title: Fournier's Gangrene
oneLiner: Necrotising fasciitis of the perineum, genitalia, or perianal region; urological emergency with mortality 20–50% — time to debridement is everything.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Septic shock** — Single most lethal immediate complication. Look for: tachycardia out of proportion to fever, hypotension (MAP <65), lactate >2 mmol/L, altered mental state. If present, start IV fluids, blood cultures, broad‑spectrum antibiotics **before** CT.
- **Gas gangrene / clostridial myonecrosis** — Rapidly spreading gas in muscle planes, associated with trauma/surgery. Differentiator: **crepitus extending beyond the perineum into the thighs or abdominal wall** — requires immediate surgical debridement and high-dose penicillin + clindamycin.
- **Testicular torsion** — Can mimic early Fournier’s in young males. Differentiator: **vertical lie of testis + absent cremasteric reflex + no perineal crepitus** — urgent scrotal Doppler ultrasound if equivocal, but do not delay Fournier’s workup if crepitus present.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Severe, unremitting perineal/scrotal pain — often out of proportion to visible findings
  - Fever, rigors, malaise
  - Dysuria, frequency, inability to void
  - Foul‑smelling discharge (if necrotic tissue present)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Erythema, oedema, warmth of scrotum/perineum
  - Crepitus on palpation (subcutaneous gas)
  - Necrotic patches, bullae, or gangrenous skin
  - Tenderness extending to abdominal wall or thigh
  - Patient may be systemically unwell (tachycardia, hypotension, altered GCS)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Perianal abscess / ischiorectal abscess** — More localised, less systemic upset, no crepitus, no rapid spread.
  - **Cellulitis of scrotum/perineum** — Erythema without crepitus or necrosis, responds to antibiotics alone.
  - **Testicular torsion** — Sudden severe testicular pain, nausea, high‑riding testis; no perineal erythema/crepitus.
  - **Idiopathic scrotal oedema** — Benign, self‑limiting, no fever, no pain out of proportion.
  - **Hernia (strangulated)** — Groin mass, bowel obstruction symptoms, no perineal crepitus.
- **Splitter:** The presence of **crepitus** (subcutaneous gas) on palpation is the single bedside test that distinguishes Fournier’s from a simple abscess or cellulitis. If crepitus is absent but strong clinical suspicion remains, proceed to CT.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Urine dip (rule out UTI as source; check for ketones if diabetic)
  - Blood glucose (diabetes is a major risk factor)
  - Assess NEWS2 and fluid responsiveness
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (WCC, platelets), U&E, creatinine, CRP, lactate, blood cultures (×2)
  - Clotting screen (if sepsis/bleeding risk)
  - Consider group & save if surgical debridement likely
- **Imaging:**
  - **First‑line:** CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast (and oral/rectal contrast if tolerated) — gold standard to confirm gas in soft tissues, define extent, identify source (colonic/perianal).
  - **Second‑line:** Plain X‑ray of pelvis/abdomen (may show subcutaneous gas, but low sensitivity); ultrasound can assess for testicular pathology but should **not** delay CT if Fournier’s suspected.
- **Special tests:** None routinely required.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** No formal validated score; diagnosis is **clinical + radiological**. The “Fournier’s Gangrene Severity Index” (FGSI) exists for prognosis (score based on temperature, HR, RR, sodium, potassium, creatinine, bicarbonate, WCC, haematocrit — score >9 associated with higher mortality) but is **not** for diagnosis.
- **Never‑miss test:** **CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast** — omission leads to delayed diagnosis, inadequate debridement, and increased mortality. If CT not immediately available, obtain a surgical opinion urgently (do not wait for imaging if clinical suspicion is high).
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Resuscitate:** IV fluids (e.g. Hartmann’s 500 mL bolus, reassess; target MAP ≥65)
  - **IV broad‑spectrum antibiotics** — start within 1 hour of suspicion (see Drugs below)
  - **Urgent surgical review** — call urology/general surgery registrar immediately; patient will require **emergent surgical debridement**
  - Insert urinary catheter (monitor output; source control if urethral involvement)
  - Analgesia (IV morphine titrated)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Admit to **ICU / HDU** for organ support (sepsis, fluid balance, vasopressors if needed)
  - **Repeat surgical debridement** every 24–48 hours until all necrotic tissue removed
  - Consider **negative pressure wound therapy** (NPWT) post‑debridement
  - ID consult for tailored antibiotics once cultures result
  - Strict input/output monitoring, daily bloods (FBC, CRP, lactate, renal function)
- **Long‑term / outpatient:**
  - Reconstructive surgery (skin grafts, scrotal reconstruction) once infection resolved
  - Consider **diverting colostomy** if rectal involvement
  - Diabetes and immunocompromise management as underlying causes
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluid: Hartmann’s or Plasmalyte at 1–2 mL/kg/hour, adjust for sepsis and renal function
  - VTE prophylaxis: low molecular weight heparin (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily) unless contraindicated (active bleeding, coagulopathy)
  - Diet: **NBM** until after surgical debridement (risk of bowel injury, ileus)
  - Observations: **NEWS2 every 1 hour** (ICU level), escalate if NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate rising
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First‑line agents (UK practice, per local policy):**
  - **Piperacillin‑tazobactam (Tazocin)** 4.5 g IV **every 6 hours** (check local policy for extended infusion)
  - **PLUS Clindamycin** 600 mg–1.2 g IV **every 6 hours** (inhibits toxin production)
  - **PLUS consider Gentamicin** (once‑daily dosing per local protocol, e.g. 5–7 mg/kg IV — [check local policy] for dosing and monitoring)
  - **OR** if penicillin allergy: **Meropenem** 1 g IV **every 8 hours** + Clindamycin (same dose)
- **Common alternatives:** Ertapenem (if mild‑moderate), or Tigecycline if multi‑drug‑resistant risk.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Piperacillin‑tazobactam: avoid if immediate hypersensitivity to penicillins; use with caution in renal impairment (dose adjust).
  - Clindamycin: may cause C. difficile colitis; caution in hepatic impairment.
  - Gentamicin: nephrotoxic, ototoxic; requires monitoring.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Piperacillin‑tazobactam: reduce dose if eGFR <40 mL/min (e.g. 4.5 g IV every 8 h if eGFR 20‑40; every 12 h if <20). [Check local policy.]
  - Meropenem: reduce if eGFR <50 mL/min.
  - Clindamycin: no dose adjustment needed for mild‑moderate renal impairment; use caution in severe hepatic disease.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Delaying the first dose** while awaiting imaging or senior review. Antibiotics must be given within 1 hour of suspicion. Also: **Under‑dosing gentamicin** (if used) — ensure weight‑based dosing and timely level monitoring.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Septic shock / multi‑organ failure
  - Requires massive debridement — loss of scrotal skin, testicular exposure, possible orchiectomy
  - Colostomy / suprapubic catheter (if urethral/rectal involvement)
  - Acute kidney injury, DIC, ARDS
- **Chronic:**
  - Extensive scarring, erectile dysfunction, urinary incontinence
  - Need for staged reconstructive surgery
  - Psychological trauma, PTSD
sections.Prognosis: - Mortality 20–50% (higher with delayed treatment, older age, diabetes, immunosuppression). Early aggressive surgical debridement is the single most important prognostic factor. If treated within 24–48 hours of symptom onset, mortality drops significantly.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always perform a perineal exam in any male with sepsis and testicular/scrotal pain** — a normal‐looking scrotum can still harbour deep infection.
- **Call the surgeon before CT if clinical suspicion is high** — they may opt for immediate exploration rather than delay.
- **Document the time of first antibiotic dose** and communicate it to the surgical team — this is a key performance indicator.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT wait for CT if crepitus is present and the patient is septic** — go straight to theatre.
- **Do NOT prescribe single‑agent antibiotics** — always use a combination covering Gram‑negatives, anaerobes, and toxin suppression (e.g. Tazocin + Clindamycin).
- **Do NOT underestimate the extent of disease** — Fournier’s spreads along fascial planes; the visible skin changes underestimate the subcutaneous infection. The surgeon must debride until healthy bleeding tissue is seen.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** This condition is **never** managed as an outpatient. Discharge is only after complete resolution of infection and wound healing. If discharged (extremely rare) with a drain or wound pack, they must return for:
  - Increasing pain, fever, or rigors
  - Inability to pass urine
  - Foul‑smelling or purulent discharge from wound
  - Worsening erythema or swelling extending to thighs/abdomen
- **Follow‑up:** Urology/general surgery outpatient clinic within 1–2 weeks; plus plastic surgery if reconstruction planned.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **When to call senior / ICU / crash team:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (start sepsis 6 pathway)
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L despite fluid resuscitation
  - GCS <15 or deteriorating
  - Need for vasopressors (MAP <65)
  - Signs of spreading infection (crepitus extending beyond perineum)
  - Any delay in getting the patient to theatre — escalate to consultant on call
- **Specific thresholds:** Use NEWS2 as per UK national early warning score; also monitor urine output (<0.5 mL/kg/hour for 2 hours suggests acute kidney injury). If any doubt, call the critical care outreach team.

rawMarkdown:
## Fournier's Gangrene
**One-liner:** Necrotising fasciitis of the perineum, genitalia, or perianal region; urological emergency with mortality 20–50% — time to debridement is everything.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Septic shock** — Single most lethal immediate complication. Look for: tachycardia out of proportion to fever, hypotension (MAP <65), lactate >2 mmol/L, altered mental state. If present, start IV fluids, blood cultures, broad‑spectrum antibiotics **before** CT.
- **Gas gangrene / clostridial myonecrosis** — Rapidly spreading gas in muscle planes, associated with trauma/surgery. Differentiator: **crepitus extending beyond the perineum into the thighs or abdominal wall** — requires immediate surgical debridement and high-dose penicillin + clindamycin.
- **Testicular torsion** — Can mimic early Fournier’s in young males. Differentiator: **vertical lie of testis + absent cremasteric reflex + no perineal crepitus** — urgent scrotal Doppler ultrasound if equivocal, but do not delay Fournier’s workup if crepitus present.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Severe, unremitting perineal/scrotal pain — often out of proportion to visible findings
  - Fever, rigors, malaise
  - Dysuria, frequency, inability to void
  - Foul‑smelling discharge (if necrotic tissue present)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Erythema, oedema, warmth of scrotum/perineum
  - Crepitus on palpation (subcutaneous gas)
  - Necrotic patches, bullae, or gangrenous skin
  - Tenderness extending to abdominal wall or thigh
  - Patient may be systemically unwell (tachycardia, hypotension, altered GCS)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Perianal abscess / ischiorectal abscess** — More localised, less systemic upset, no crepitus, no rapid spread.
  - **Cellulitis of scrotum/perineum** — Erythema without crepitus or necrosis, responds to antibiotics alone.
  - **Testicular torsion** — Sudden severe testicular pain, nausea, high‑riding testis; no perineal erythema/crepitus.
  - **Idiopathic scrotal oedema** — Benign, self‑limiting, no fever, no pain out of proportion.
  - **Hernia (strangulated)** — Groin mass, bowel obstruction symptoms, no perineal crepitus.
- **Splitter:** The presence of **crepitus** (subcutaneous gas) on palpation is the single bedside test that distinguishes Fournier’s from a simple abscess or cellulitis. If crepitus is absent but strong clinical suspicion remains, proceed to CT.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Urine dip (rule out UTI as source; check for ketones if diabetic)
  - Blood glucose (diabetes is a major risk factor)
  - Assess NEWS2 and fluid responsiveness
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (WCC, platelets), U&E, creatinine, CRP, lactate, blood cultures (×2)
  - Clotting screen (if sepsis/bleeding risk)
  - Consider group & save if surgical debridement likely
- **Imaging:**
  - **First‑line:** CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast (and oral/rectal contrast if tolerated) — gold standard to confirm gas in soft tissues, define extent, identify source (colonic/perianal).
  - **Second‑line:** Plain X‑ray of pelvis/abdomen (may show subcutaneous gas, but low sensitivity); ultrasound can assess for testicular pathology but should **not** delay CT if Fournier’s suspected.
- **Special tests:** None routinely required.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** No formal validated score; diagnosis is **clinical + radiological**. The “Fournier’s Gangrene Severity Index” (FGSI) exists for prognosis (score based on temperature, HR, RR, sodium, potassium, creatinine, bicarbonate, WCC, haematocrit — score >9 associated with higher mortality) but is **not** for diagnosis.
- **Never‑miss test:** **CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast** — omission leads to delayed diagnosis, inadequate debridement, and increased mortality. If CT not immediately available, obtain a surgical opinion urgently (do not wait for imaging if clinical suspicion is high).

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Resuscitate:** IV fluids (e.g. Hartmann’s 500 mL bolus, reassess; target MAP ≥65)
  - **IV broad‑spectrum antibiotics** — start within 1 hour of suspicion (see Drugs below)
  - **Urgent surgical review** — call urology/general surgery registrar immediately; patient will require **emergent surgical debridement**
  - Insert urinary catheter (monitor output; source control if urethral involvement)
  - Analgesia (IV morphine titrated)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Admit to **ICU / HDU** for organ support (sepsis, fluid balance, vasopressors if needed)
  - **Repeat surgical debridement** every 24–48 hours until all necrotic tissue removed
  - Consider **negative pressure wound therapy** (NPWT) post‑debridement
  - ID consult for tailored antibiotics once cultures result
  - Strict input/output monitoring, daily bloods (FBC, CRP, lactate, renal function)
- **Long‑term / outpatient:**
  - Reconstructive surgery (skin grafts, scrotal reconstruction) once infection resolved
  - Consider **diverting colostomy** if rectal involvement
  - Diabetes and immunocompromise management as underlying causes
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluid: Hartmann’s or Plasmalyte at 1–2 mL/kg/hour, adjust for sepsis and renal function
  - VTE prophylaxis: low molecular weight heparin (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily) unless contraindicated (active bleeding, coagulopathy)
  - Diet: **NBM** until after surgical debridement (risk of bowel injury, ileus)
  - Observations: **NEWS2 every 1 hour** (ICU level), escalate if NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate rising

### Drugs & Doses
- **First‑line agents (UK practice, per local policy):**
  - **Piperacillin‑tazobactam (Tazocin)** 4.5 g IV **every 6 hours** (check local policy for extended infusion)
  - **PLUS Clindamycin** 600 mg–1.2 g IV **every 6 hours** (inhibits toxin production)
  - **PLUS consider Gentamicin** (once‑daily dosing per local protocol, e.g. 5–7 mg/kg IV — [check local policy] for dosing and monitoring)
  - **OR** if penicillin allergy: **Meropenem** 1 g IV **every 8 hours** + Clindamycin (same dose)
- **Common alternatives:** Ertapenem (if mild‑moderate), or Tigecycline if multi‑drug‑resistant risk.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Piperacillin‑tazobactam: avoid if immediate hypersensitivity to penicillins; use with caution in renal impairment (dose adjust).
  - Clindamycin: may cause C. difficile colitis; caution in hepatic impairment.
  - Gentamicin: nephrotoxic, ototoxic; requires monitoring.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Piperacillin‑tazobactam: reduce dose if eGFR <40 mL/min (e.g. 4.5 g IV every 8 h if eGFR 20‑40; every 12 h if <20). [Check local policy.]
  - Meropenem: reduce if eGFR <50 mL/min.
  - Clindamycin: no dose adjustment needed for mild‑moderate renal impairment; use caution in severe hepatic disease.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Delaying the first dose** while awaiting imaging or senior review. Antibiotics must be given within 1 hour of suspicion. Also: **Under‑dosing gentamicin** (if used) — ensure weight‑based dosing and timely level monitoring.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Septic shock / multi‑organ failure
  - Requires massive debridement — loss of scrotal skin, testicular exposure, possible orchiectomy
  - Colostomy / suprapubic catheter (if urethral/rectal involvement)
  - Acute kidney injury, DIC, ARDS
- **Chronic:**
  - Extensive scarring, erectile dysfunction, urinary incontinence
  - Need for staged reconstructive surgery
  - Psychological trauma, PTSD

### Prognosis
- Mortality 20–50% (higher with delayed treatment, older age, diabetes, immunosuppression). Early aggressive surgical debridement is the single most important prognostic factor. If treated within 24–48 hours of symptom onset, mortality drops significantly.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always perform a perineal exam in any male with sepsis and testicular/scrotal pain** — a normal‐looking scrotum can still harbour deep infection.
- **Call the surgeon before CT if clinical suspicion is high** — they may opt for immediate exploration rather than delay.
- **Document the time of first antibiotic dose** and communicate it to the surgical team — this is a key performance indicator.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT wait for CT if crepitus is present and the patient is septic** — go straight to theatre.
- **Do NOT prescribe single‑agent antibiotics** — always use a combination covering Gram‑negatives, anaerobes, and toxin suppression (e.g. Tazocin + Clindamycin).
- **Do NOT underestimate the extent of disease** — Fournier’s spreads along fascial planes; the visible skin changes underestimate the subcutaneous infection. The surgeon must debride until healthy bleeding tissue is seen.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** This condition is **never** managed as an outpatient. Discharge is only after complete resolution of infection and wound healing. If discharged (extremely rare) with a drain or wound pack, they must return for:
  - Increasing pain, fever, or rigors
  - Inability to pass urine
  - Foul‑smelling or purulent discharge from wound
  - Worsening erythema or swelling extending to thighs/abdomen
- **Follow‑up:** Urology/general surgery outpatient clinic within 1–2 weeks; plus plastic surgery if reconstruction planned.

### Escalation Criteria
- **When to call senior / ICU / crash team:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (start sepsis 6 pathway)
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L despite fluid resuscitation
  - GCS <15 or deteriorating
  - Need for vasopressors (MAP <65)
  - Signs of spreading infection (crepitus extending beyond perineum)
  - Any delay in getting the patient to theatre — escalate to consultant on call
- **Specific thresholds:** Use NEWS2 as per UK national early warning score; also monitor urine output (<0.5 mL/kg/hour for 2 hours suggests acute kidney injury). If any doubt, call the critical care outreach team.
